--- SOURCE 4: Strategic evaluation of rural-to-urban migration trends and policies ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X

SUMMARY:
This study strategically evaluates rural-to-urban migration trends and policies in Türkiye using Multi-Criteria Decision-Making (MCDM) methods under Fermatean fuzzy logic. While FF-AHP is applied to evaluate factors for rural-to-urban migration, FF-EDAS assesses effective practices that will support rural areas and prevent irregular migration to the cities in Türkiye. *   •It provides a detailed multi-criteria analysis of rural-to-urban migration factors and strategic policy options in Türkiye, a context that previous studies have not examined using an integrated decision-making approach. E.M. Dokubo, S.O. Sennuga, A.F. Omolayo, O.L. Bankole  Effect of Rural-Urban Migration Among the Youths and Its Impacts on Agricultural Development in Kuje Area Council, 4, Science and Technology, Abuja, Nigeria (2023), pp. 12-27 Google Scholar.%20Effect%20of%20Rural-Urban%20Migration%20Among%20the%20Youths%20and%20Its%20Impacts%20on%20Agricultural%20Development%20in%20Kuje%20Area%20Council%2C%20Abuja%2C%20Nigeria.%20Science%20and%20Technology%2C%204(2)%2C%2012%E2%80%9327.)

Strategic evaluation of rural-to-urban migration trends and policies in Türkiye
===============================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Sukran Seker a, Ertugrul Ayyildiz b c, Nezir Aydin a b

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.sftr.2025.100875](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.sftr.2025.100875 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S266618882500440X&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

Open access

Abstract
--------

Rural-urban migration refers to the movement of people from rural to urban areas. This study strategically evaluates rural-to-urban migration trends and policies in [Türkiye](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/turkey "Learn more about Türkiye from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") using Multi-Criteria Decision-Making (MCDM) methods under Fermatean fuzzy logic. The main and sub-factors influencing rural-to-urban are identified and structured under the PESTELT (Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, Legal, and Transportation) framework. It identifies key drivers of rural-to-urban migration, such as employment opportunities and educational access, rather than solely focusing on the cost of living. The goal is to develop strategies that encourage reverse migration back to rural areas. Using integrated Fermatean Fuzzy-Analytical Hierarchy Process (FF-AHP) and Fermatean Fuzzy-Evaluation based on Distance from Average Solution (FF-EDAS) methods, the study prioritizes strategies like regional employment investments and promoting village industries to strengthen rural settlement. Findings offer policy insights to balance urban-rural population distribution sustainably.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825004319)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825002503)

Keywords
--------

Internal migration

Rural-to-urban migration

Fermatean fuzzy numbers

AHP

EDAS

1. Introduction
---------------

Migration refers to the movement of people from their usual place of residence to a new location. This could be within a country, known as internal migration, or it could be across national borders, known as [international migration](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/international-migration "Learn more about international migration from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The phenomenon of internal migration encompasses rural-to-urban shifts, which are of particular significance in demographic and socio-economic studies [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0100)]. Rural-to-urban migration impacts the socioeconomic dynamics of municipalities and, subsequently, the overall characteristics of [urban life](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/urban-sociology "Learn more about urban life from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0060)]. Rural-to-urban migration is frequently driven by the desire to enhance livelihood opportunities and expand [living standards](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/living-standard "Learn more about living standards from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This highlights the importance of designing [urban environments](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/sustainable-urban-development "Learn more about urban environments from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that facilitate [sustainable development](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/environmental-impact-assessment "Learn more about sustainable development from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [wealth](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/wealth "Learn more about wealth from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). While planning, designing, or reconstructing an urban area, the researchers have highlighted several factors to keep urban life alive and reduce the desire for such migration. In this sense, some of the factors that need to be considered are employment opportunities [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0019),[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0040)], revenue generation prospects [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0040)], [financial services](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/financial-services "Learn more about financial services from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") accessibility [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0025)], education prospects [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0042),[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0083)], cultural diversity [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0085)], tourism potential [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0079)] and socialization opportunities. Furthermore, a transparent and intrinsic relationship exists between the dynamics of migration and the quality of urban local services and infrastructure, such as the quality of health services [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0020),[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0032)], [waste management](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/waste-management "Learn more about waste management from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0078)], climatic conditions and [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0035), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0064), [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0077)], and the readiness to disasters [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0076)]. Besides the conditions and infrastructure of an urban area, some governmental decisions also affect migration decisions. For instance, government regulations [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0025)], tax policies [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0043),[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0051)], [natural resource management](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/natural-resources-management "Learn more about natural resource management from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0071)], and public transportation policies [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0007)]. Indeed, every aspect plays a focal role in shaping the attraction and livability of an urban area and managing rural-to-urban migration more healthily. Moreover, in an era where rapid technological progression is the norm, factors such as access to media, the availability of micro-mobility choices, and air, sea, and [railway networks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/railway-network "Learn more about railway networks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are emerging as critical bases of an urban area's attractiveness. In addition, traffic congestion levels emphasize the need for effective [road networks](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/road-network "Learn more about road networks from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and accessibility to public transportation options to alleviate urban mobility encounters [[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0038),[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0070)]. Rural-to-urban migration triggers precise imbalances in the population formation of settlements, e.g., in terms of age and gender, as well as economic, social, and cultural changes in the settlements. This movement also causes the emergence of infrastructural inadequacies, unregistered employment, violence, unused cultivated terrain, and weak [livestock](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/farm-animal "Learn more about livestock from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") activities. Achieving a balance in such an internal migration process is crucial to eliminate these adverse situations.

This paper investigates the key factors driving rural-to-urban migration in [Türkiye](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/turkey "Learn more about Türkiye from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and aims to evaluate strategies and policies designed to manage, balance, and reduce migration rates from rural to urban areas. Furthermore, this paper seeks to discover critical aspects for people who want to migrate from their hometowns. In the perception of the complex interrelatedness among inner relocation and the several attributes that involve urban life, this research incorporates a widespread exhibit of connected factors. These incorporate concerns about the quality of urban planning, the application of equal opportunity strategies, resident intensity management, and crime [prevention strategies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/prevention-strategies "Learn more about prevention strategies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") among people.

Several methods may be applied to identify factors, but a structured method is needed for such a complex problem. Consequently, these conflicting factors are subjected to an examination and categorization process. For this purpose, the [PESTELT](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/political-economics "Learn more about PESTELT from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") approach was proposed as an extension of the PEST analysis. PEST, initially developed in 1967 by Harvard professor Francis Aguilar, was designed to assist organizations in identifying and evaluating potential threats and opportunities for their corporations [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0003)]. In this study, an extended version of the traditional PEST analysis, named PESTELT, is utilized to comprehensively structure the factors influencing rural-to-urban migration. PESTELT stands for Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, Legal, and Transportation dimensions. This extension was made to enable a more detailed and lower-level analysis of factors that are critical in shaping rural-to-urban migration trends. Accordingly, the identified migration-related factors were systematically categorized under seven primary categories and 27 corresponding sub-factors.

Evaluating the most impactful factors that represent the attractiveness of an urban area includes conflicting assessments. Thus, decision-makers [face](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/face "Learn more about face from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") a complex problem. In such a problem, not only are interrelated factors considered, but uncertainty is also in question. Thus, in this study, we proposed an integrated fuzzy-based multicriteria decision-making (MCDM) framework to make such decisions based on two well-known [MCDM](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/multi-criteria-decision-making "Learn more about MCDM from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") approaches. The study integrated the [Analytical Hierarchy Process](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/analytical-hierarchy-process "Learn more about Analytical Hierarchy Process from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (AHP) and Evaluation based on distance from average solution (EDAS). Fermatean Fuzzy Numbers (FFNs) [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0024),[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0073)] are applied to handle uncertainty caused by subjective evaluations made by experts. When decisions are contingent upon resolving conflicting factors, applying traditional fuzzy sets may prove inadequate. For instance, if the constraints imposed upon the sum and quadratic sum of membership and non-membership degrees, which preclude these values from exceeding 1, can impede the formulation of well-founded decisions. Conversely, FFSs lack these limitations, conferring greater flexibility in decision-making assessments. In FFSs, the sum of cubes of membership and non-membership degrees varies between 0 and 1, offering decision-makers a wider range of possibilities [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0075)]. Therefore, FFSs are preferred in this research. FF-AHP was used to determine the weights of the factors that affect the decision to integrate migration, and FF-EDAS was used to evaluate the local development strategies.

Considering the literature, rural-to-urban migration in Türkiye has not been analyzed at a detailed level using an [MCDM](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/multiple-criteria-decision-analysis "Learn more about MCDM from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") method to evaluate factors for rural to urban areas and create and analyze strategies for governments. To the authors' best knowledge, this is the first MCDM study that integrates FF-AHP and FF-EDAS methods. While FF-AHP is applied to evaluate factors for rural-to-urban migration, FF-EDAS assesses effective practices that will support rural areas and prevent irregular migration to the cities in Türkiye. In evaluating factors, the PEST analysis was extended to PESTELT, the first application of this extension, and PESTELT analysis was used to determine the factors to be considered while assessing the strategies.

Further, the following research questions were investigated:
*   •What are the most influential factors driving rural-to-urban migration in Türkiye? 
*   •Which strategic [development policies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/policy-development "Learn more about development policies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can most effectively support rural regions and reduce the pressures of such internal migration? 
*   •How can integrated Fermatean fuzzy MCDM methods (FF-AHP and FF-EDAS) provide a robust framework for assessing influential factors and strategies under uncertainty? 

In summary, the contributions of this study are:
*   •It provides a detailed multi-criteria analysis of rural-to-urban migration factors and strategic policy options in Türkiye, a context that previous studies have not examined using an integrated decision-making approach. 
*   •It introduces a novel methodology by integrating FF- AHP and FF-EDAS, marking the first use of this hybrid MCDM framework in the literature. 
*   •It extends the traditional PEST framework to PESTELT for categorizing migration drivers, representing the first application of this extended factor framework and enabling a more comprehensive evaluation of the influences on rural-to-urban migration. 

In the rest of the study, a literature review is provided in [Section 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#sec0002), and [Section 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#sec0003) gives the developed methodology in detail. [Section 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#sec0007) provides details of the application, such as results and discussions. In [Section 5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#sec0010), managerial implications are presented, and in the last section ([Section 6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#sec0011)), concluding remarks, limitations, and future directions are given.

2. Literature review
--------------------

Migration is seen as one of the main reasons for population change, along with “fertility” and “mortality” [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0098)]. Rural-to-urban migration, as an internal migration, includes two main concerns: who migrates from their region and which regions` population increases. The first concern is linked with the economic activities, sociocultural conditions, and [governmental policies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/governmental-policy "Learn more about governmental policies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), while the second concern highlights the regions that have advantages in terms of economic, demographic dynamics, and policies [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0098),[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0101)].

Lucas [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0054)] attributes factors that cause such internal migration, including employment opportunities, earnings differences, job opportunities, and education. Above all, Lucas [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0097)] detailed the challenges to determining the real cause of internal migration within developing countries and highlighted the lack of data availability. Rees et al. [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0069)] investigated the relationship between migration and population density between regions of countries, comparing by regression to show the direction and speed of population density. In this context, a study is presented that includes developed countries (in Europe, North America, and Oceania) and less developed countries, which have a total number of countries that host four-fifths of the world's population. By examining the correlation between population distribution and national development factors, they proposed a model that reveals how rural-to-urban migration affects the system in the development process. Besides economic, sociocultural, or [governmental policies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/governmental-policy "Learn more about governmental policies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), several other reasons for rural-to-urban migration have been investigated by researchers. For instance, Mastrorillo et al. [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0057)] investigated the influence of climate fluctuation on such migration streams in Southern Africa. A model was used that included [climate variability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/climate-variation "Learn more about climate variability from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and a range of geographical, demographic, and socio-economic components conventionally detected as prospective drivers of migration. Extreme temperatures and excessive rainfall have been found to have a driving effect and increase out-migration. O’Brien et al. looked into such migration from a different perspective. They explained how rural-to-urban migration in Eastern Europe, especially in rural Banat, Romania, has been shaped by post-1989 changes and past communist-era policies. They highlighted that the local resentment toward migrants, persistent stereotypes, and tensions between preserving local culture and keeping small communities viable amid depopulation [[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0099)].

Beyond these broader studies, several researchers have investigated internal migration in specific country contexts using various analytical approaches. Rashid utilized the geographic information system (GIS) based [MCDM](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/multi-criteria-decision-making "Learn more about MCDM from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") methodology to formulate the internal migration potential model [[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0068)]. Inalou et al. discovered the factors related to sustainable small-city development to analyze the population relocation from countryside settlements to bigger municipalities in Iran [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0040)]. Chan and Lee prioritized the [sustainable city](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/sustainable-urban-development "Learn more about sustainable city from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") factor and determined migration as an essential factor in Cambodia [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0019)]. Chen et al. [[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0022)] presented underlying facts on the influence of retirement on internal migration in a developing country (China). They concluded that removing immigration restrictions and improving government benefits to retirees can help a developing country cope with [population aging](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/ageing-population "Learn more about population aging from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

To address this complexity, researchers have turned to [MCDM](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/multiple-criteria-decision-analysis "Learn more about MCDM from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") methods to better capture and evaluate the numerous factors involved. [AHP](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/analytical-hierarchy-process "Learn more about AHP from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a well-known MCDM technique and is extended with different fuzzy sets. FF-AHP effectively models information's fuzziness and ambiguity in complex decision-making problems. Zeng et al. introduced the FF-AHP to evaluate the quality of online teaching platforms [[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0092)]. Alkan and Kahraman [[37](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0004)] extended the [AHP](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/economics-econometrics-and-finance/ahp-approach "Learn more about AHP from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") with interval-valued fermatean fuzzy numbers (IVFFNs) to analyze the digital transformation strategies for [supply chains](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/supply-chain-management "Learn more about supply chains from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Bouraima et al. determined the weights of accident causes for construction site projects [[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0016)]. Suvitha et al. [[39](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0081)] integrated [AHP](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/analytical-hierarchy-process "Learn more about AHP from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and CODAS to determine the best plastic [waste management](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/waste-management "Learn more about waste management from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") systems using the Fermatean probabilistic hesitant fuzzy set to model the fuzziness. EDAS is a distance-based decision-making method. EDAS and its extensions with different fuzzy sets have a wide application area in the literature. Mishra et al. extended EDAS with CRITIC for sustainable third-party reverse logistics provider selection problems under a fermatean fuzzy environment [[40](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0059)]. Zeng et al. evaluated the green suppliers with FF-EDAS [[41](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S266618882500440X#bib0091)]. AHP and EDAS were integrated with different purposes in the current decision-making literature. Karatop et al. evalua

# Migration-Informed Urban Planning - A 3 Step Guidebook from the Urban Planning and Infrastructure in Migration Contexts (UPIMC) Programme in Cameroon, Egypt, and Jordan

In response to the increasing challenges posed by migration and displacement in urban areas, the Migration Informed Urban Planning: A 3-Step Guidebook provides a comprehensive methodology for integrating migrants and displaced persons into urban planning processes. This guidebook, developed under the Urban Planning and Infrastructure in Migration Contexts (UPIMC) programme, is designed to support urban planners, governments, international organizations and other stakeholders in creating inclusive and resilient cities.

The publication is built on experiences and lessons learned from pilot projects in Cameroon, Egypt, and Jordan, offering practical tools and best practices for addressing urban migration challenges. It outlines a three-phased methodology: “Understand the City,” “Planning the City,” and “Transforming the City”, to develop sustainable urban planning frameworks that effectively incorporate the needs of migrants and displaced populations and their host communities in the most vulnerable neighbourhoods.

The guidebook not only provides actionable strategies but also highlights successful interventions, offering recommendations for replicating these approaches in similar contexts. It aims to enhance the capacity of local governments and communities to proactively address urban migration issues, fostering social cohesion and sustainable development in rapidly growing urban areas.

This publication is an essential resource for urban planners, development practitioners, and policymakers working in cities facing significant migration pressures. It is also of interest to donors, the private sector, and actors involved in financing sustainable and inclusive development in cities, offering valuable insights and tools to support inclusive and resilient urban growth.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Implement inclusive urban planning that integrates migrant needs, promotes sustainable infrastructure, and enhances accessibility, ensuring balanced development in response to rural-urban migration trends.
Establish comprehensive support systems, including legal assistance, housing programs, and social services, to facilitate migrants' integration and improve their quality of life in urban areas.
Invest in rural development initiatives to enhance local economies, create job opportunities, and improve living standards, thereby reducing the impetus for rural-urban migration.
